trajectory prediction [14]. In contrast to prior methodologies
using explicit representations for extracting trajectory features,
the objective of this article is to leverage implicit repre-
sentations for modeling the diffusion of motion uncertainty,
thereby shaping stochastic trajectory predictions. In addition,
in the domain of pedestrian trajectory prediction, construct-
ing effective datasets and proposing corresponding solutions
represent the primary steps in addressing challenges. Ini-
tially, we investigated the application of pedestrian trajectory
prediction methods in campus and surrounding scenarios.
The continuous evolution of autonomous driving technology
has introduced new demands, such as the navigation of
autonomous delivery vehicles and courier vehicles in intricate
and congested environments. These settings are characterized
by high pedestrian traffic, chaos, irregular road structures,
and relatively narrow pathways. However, we observed a
notable absence of pedestrian trajectory datasets tailored
to these specific scenarios, presenting an opportunity for
in-depth exploration. To address this research gap, this article
introduces a novel dataset, referred to as VPT. Distinguishing
itself from the existing studies, our dataset is collected within
the campus and surrounding environments through digital twin
technology [15], [16], aligning more closely with practical
applications and establishing a foundation for the future appli-
cation of autonomous driving technology in such intricate
settings.

particularly in fields such as image generation, have been
